_note: Evergreen analogy cards that translate soccer ideas into language American sports fans already understand.

cards.title: A press is soccer's version of a full-court press
cards.hook: The point is not random hustle. The point is to make the next pass ugly.
cards.summary: When a team presses, it pushes up together to trap the opponent before the attack can settle. Think basketball defenders swarming a ball-handler and taking away the easy outlet.
cards.whyItMatters: New fans often see frantic running and miss that it is coordinated pressure with a map behind it.
cards.whatToWatch: See whether the first attacker angles the play toward one sideline, where the rest of the team can spring the trap.

cards.title: A counterattack is the closest thing soccer has to a fast break
cards.hook: The danger spikes in the seconds before the other team can get organized.
cards.summary: A counterattack starts the moment possession changes hands and one team races into the space the opponent just left behind. It feels a lot like a live-ball turnover in basketball that instantly becomes a numbers advantage the other way.
cards.whyItMatters: This helps American fans understand why a team can look calm without the ball and still feel dangerous all night.
cards.whatToWatch: After a turnover, count how many defenders are still goal-side of the ball. If the answer is not many, the break is on.

cards.title: A set piece is like a scripted red-zone possession
cards.hook: Everyone knows the ball is about to arrive in a dangerous spot, so preparation matters more than surprise.
cards.summary: Corners and attacking free kicks are rehearsed moments. They are soccer's closest equivalent to an NFL team calling a designed play near the goal line, where blocking, timing, and one matchup can decide everything.
cards.whyItMatters: Tournament matches are tight, so teams that are average in open play can still steal huge moments from dead balls.
cards.whatToWatch: Notice the decoy runs, screens, near-post darts, and the one player trying to arrive late into the real scoring lane.

cards.title: A 1-0 match is closer to a pitchers' duel than a broken offense
cards.hook: Low scoring does not mean nothing is happening.
cards.summary: Soccer has fewer scoring events than basketball or football, so a 1-0 game can still be sharp, tense, and high quality. Think baseball when one mistake, one hanging pitch, or one perfect swing ends up deciding the whole night.
cards.whyItMatters: This resets expectations for fans who are waiting for constant points instead of constant tension.
cards.whatToWatch: Look for field position, dangerous entries into the box, and how each team handles the one or two clean chances it may actually get.

cards.title: Extra time is overtime with tired legs and no reset button
cards.hook: The game keeps going, but the players do not magically get fresh.
cards.summary: When a knockout match goes to extra time, both teams keep playing after regulation instead of starting a brand-new contest. It is a little like overtime in any American sport, except everyone is already carrying ninety minutes of physical and emotional wear.
cards.whyItMatters: That fatigue changes everything: decision-making slows, space opens up, and even favorites can start managing risk instead of chasing beauty.
cards.whatToWatch: Watch the body language of fullbacks and midfielders first. They are usually the ones showing the strain before the scoreboard does.
